data.epi_content:
If any devout human were listening to this conversation, they would surely point fingers at me for daring to insult God, making my already non-existent reputation even worse.

God... Before waking up in this place, I had never once been to church, and all I knew about religion or doctrine was what I was forced to hear from a Christian friend who fervently believed.

Yet most people in this world believe in a monotheistic God.

Thanks to that, I'm also forced to be dragged to some religious facility within the Academy once a week, whether it's a church or cathedral—not that it really matters.

While I was wondering why I had to endure this nonsense, I saw an annoyingly handsome man leading the service and realized something.

All these fancy, unnecessary settings were for the story between that handsome man who claims to deliver God's word and Vivian.

Of course, there's no way the benevolent God he speaks of actually exists.

You know how romance novels always have one of these characters?

In male-oriented stories, it's a beautiful holy woman, and in female-oriented ones, a handsome holy man.

Anyway, from what I gather from his sermon, those who don't believe in God are uncivilized and not part of civilization, therefore not human.

And the reason some nobles and royals receive different treatment is because God has chosen to bestow special grace upon them.

So in this world where everyone is supposedly equal under God, there are still slaves, commoners, and nobles who use magic.

Everything that happens in the world is already predetermined by the omnipotent God, so we must always believe in and follow Him.

If that's the case, should I just accept that being dragged down and suffering all sorts of injustices is simply God's plan for me?

It feels like he's speaking directly to me who fell into this world, making me feel resigned to my fate.

"Vivian."

"...Yes."

"I'll decline your dinner invitation. And please don't come looking for me out of concern.

I'm not your friend. Actually, why do you even come looking for me when you have so many impressive friends?"

I ask the part I understand least.

But Vivian just sniffles, showing no intention of answering, so I sigh once.

"...I think it's natural to approach someone you want to be friends with, regardless of whether they're impressive or not..."

"That's quite natural in dreams and fairy tales full of hope.

But in reality, people naturally measure various things when meeting others."

If I hadn't died, there probably would have been a scene in the novel where Vivian and I met.

Instead of speaking calmly without raising my voice like now, I would have displayed all sorts of emotions in an ugly, stubborn, and unpleasant manner, unlike how I've lived my life until now.

"And Vivian, I don't like you."

"Yes, I suppose you wouldn't."

The response came not in a woman's voice.

When I turned my head, I saw Evan holding a book, apparently having overheard our conversation.

He was leaning against the wall in a relaxed posture but ready to move at any moment, as if thinking I might slap her.

"Erica, why can't you just get along with each other?

Have you ever seen our parents treat people they dislike with anything but smiles? Even when someone sent assassins after them, they still maintained pleasant faces..."

I cut off Evan's nonsense.

"Well, that family has been burned to the ground and collapsed for quite some time now.

I don't understand what you're talking about."

When I said this with a smile, he shut his mouth.

Looking slightly taken aback.

People who grow up only looking at books and their parents are usually like that.

They focus so much on one thing that they push everything else aside and can't respond properly.
data.epi_content2:
When something unexpected happens, they just get flustered and do nothing.

I don't want to make assumptions, but it's just experience, experience.

I turned to Vivian and said:

"I don't think the children who disliked you when you were young disliked you for the same reasons I do."

They probably did, since all the boys in town would have had something resembling romantic feelings for you.

"Still, since I'm a reasonable person, I'll tell you why I dislike you."

I talk about disliking emotions while claiming to be reasonable.

This sounds like something that would make a hunchbacked garter belt inventor who's never left his birthplace snicker and cackle.

Ah, even this waiting-for-an-answer look is cute. It makes me jealous.

It's ridiculous how I tried to pick a fight and somehow separate her from Evan.

If I had just told her to stay away from Evan and me, would she have listened?

Well, that's a meaningless assumption anyway.

Evan wouldn't have stopped meeting Vivian because of what I said, and Vivian would have continued approaching me like she does now.

Evan looks curious too, tilting his head.

He probably thinks the situation isn't that serious, especially since I'm being calm, so it must not be a big deal.

Right, the ending should be clean.

As always, the person who got angry in front of Vivian before wasn't me.

"The reason... you mean?"

Because I would never be so undignified, unrefined, impolite, disrespectful, or violent.

It suddenly occurred to me.

Maybe I've been making up reasons to dislike Vivian—because she enchanted Evan, because she seemed to look down on me, because of her lower status.

Should I just lie?

To tell the truth, I don't think I really hate Vivian.

As I've said repeatedly, to the point of tedium, who could hate this lovable girl?

I might be jealous, but that's as far as it goes.

I'm just admiring beauty, like looking at a masterpiece on the wall where the artist's soul has been ground away.

After saying I would explain my reasons, I fell silent, and Vivian looked slightly puzzled.

I curl up the corners of my mouth, take a breath, and deliver a sneer, gathering the most negative expression I can muster.

"You might be wondering why I'm not speaking.

But how can I explain when there's no reason, heh."

Well, she doesn't cry.

Of course not—grown people wouldn't cry over such an exchange.

"No matter how delicious the cookies you bring, how kindly you treat me, how late at night you come to check on me, or how you come like now wanting to be friends again and apologize for any wrongdoing—no matter what you do, I think I'll still dislike you.

Just like no matter what I say to Evan, he likes you, not me.

Vivian, I truly, terribly..."

Of course, as soon as class ended and lunch break began, he must have been looking all over for Vivian before finding her like this.

"Erica Mecklenburg, stop right there."

"Don't interrupt me. I still haven't—no, never mind."

And if it looks like Vivian might get hurt, he even covers my mouth—who would deny that Evan likes Vivian?
data.epi_content3:
"Why, that name attached to this young lady will soon disappear.

It would be better to just call her by her name, but I guess our bookworm can't even think of that."

"How can you say such..."

Evan looks bewildered, confused—same difference.

He had that same expression when I fell on him after climbing a tree.

"I don't want to hear that from someone who knows nothing but books.

You dislike me so much that you'd tear me apart with words, just... um."

I bit my lip.

It bleeds slightly.

I can feel it flowing.

Maybe I'm drooling while thinking this.

When emotions rise, it becomes really difficult to control the body.

My speech pattern isn't coming out in that lady-like manner.

Has it run away somewhere?

It doesn't matter though.

Well, I can do it myself.

Mimicking someone else's speech pattern is quite simple.

"I'm having trouble speaking. I'm not trying to fight. I'm sorry. Just sorry for everything."

And then I smiled.

Perhaps emotions welled up so much that even though I wouldn't have been disturbed by being burned with a match, something like this...

"...Erica."

Evan responds.

He's calling my name.

Even though he calls it.

Well, living like this wouldn't be so bad.

There's not much time left anyway.

Thinking this, I spoke, and the words came out normally.

"Evan, and Vivian. Just don't approach me.

Leave me alone, that's enough. Really, that's all I need."

Whether it's sadness, pain, anger, or misery.

Expanding on these emotions seems meaningless now.

After some time passes, it simply becomes empty due to its simplicity.

I glanced at Evan once.

He still looks uninterested.

He seems a bit flustered, but that's all.

There's not a single string attached anyway.

It seems like there's no string connecting me to the world except for this body.

While other people have numerous strings holding them to the world.

I only have one, so cutting it would be very simple.

To let Evan and Vivian remain in their awkward atmosphere, I left with a slightly mischievous thought.

And now I'm returning to my room to prepare for a journey.
